title: Documentation
description: Language reference, design documents, and contributor guides for Elle

content.text: These documents define Elle's syntax, semantics, and type system.

rows: Complete language reference: syntax, data types, variables, functions, control flow, scoping
Type system: the mutable/immutable split, all types, predicates, display format, equality
Authoritative semantics: truthiness, lists, conditionals, equality, destructuring
Macro system: current state, architecture, hygiene, scope sets
Module system: closure-as-module, parametric imports, qualified symbols, trade-offs

content.text: Architecture and design rationale for Elle's core systems.

rows: Signal system: motivation, signal protocol, signal inference, JIT integration
Fiber architecture: execution contexts, signals, suspension/resumption, parent/child chains
FFI design: type descriptors, signatures, calling C functions, callbacks, marshalling

content.text: Practical guides for working on the Elle codebase.

rows: Step-by-step recipes for common changes: new primitives, heap types, bytecode instructions, special forms, lint rules, macros
Testing strategy: decision tree, test categories, property tests, CI structure, running tests
Compilation pipeline: entry points, VM ownership, expander lifecycle, fixpoint loop, caching
Debugging toolkit: introspection primitives, time API, signal system, memory profiling
Intentional design oddities and their rationale

content.text: Signals are typed, cooperative flow-control interrupts. A signal is a keyword — `:error`, `:log`, `:abort`, or any user-defined name — that a fiber emits to its parent. The parent's signal mask determines which signals surface; unmasked signals propagate further up.
The compiler infers which functions can emit signals and enforces that silent contexts don't call yielding ones.

# Declare a user-defined signal
(signal :abort)

# A function that signals early termination
(defn find-first [pred xs]
  (each x in xs
    (when (pred x)
      (emit :abort x))))

# Drive it from a fiber
(def f (fiber/new (fn [] (find-first even? [1 3 5 4 7])) |:abort|))
(fiber/resume f)
(print "found:" (fiber/value f))  # => found: 4

A fiber is an independent execution context — its own stack, call frames, signal mask, and heap. Fibers are cooperative and explicitly resumed. The parent drives execution by calling `fiber/resume`. When a fiber emits a signal, it suspends and the parent decides what to do next.
When a fiber finishes, its entire heap is freed in O(1) — no GC pause, no reference counting.

# A fiber yields values one at a time
(defn produce []
  (emit :yield 1)
  (emit :yield 2)
  (emit :yield 3))

(def f (fiber/new produce |:yield|))

(fiber/resume f) (print (fiber/value f))  # => 1
(fiber/resume f) (print (fiber/value f))  # => 2
(fiber/resume f) (print (fiber/value f))  # => 3
Elle has fully hygienic macros built on scope sets. Macros run at compile time and produce syntax objects that respect lexical scope.

# Define a macro that times an expression
(defmacro time-it [expr]
  (def g (gensym))
  ~(let ((,g (clock/now)))
     ,expr
     (print "elapsed:" (- (clock/now) ,g))))

(time-it (fold + 0 (range 10000)))
Elle can call C functions directly. `ffi/native` loads shared libraries and `ffi/defbind` creates typed bindings with automatic marshalling.

# Load libc and call puts
(def libc (ffi/native nil))
(ffi/defbind libc puts [:string] :int)
(puts "hello from C")
